affordances.name: Costly reciprocal value trust test
affordances.mechanism: When trust must be built before a difficult ask, negotiation, or sale can be evaluated, require the answer to test whether the value offered first is real, relevant, and costly rather than obligation theater.

use_when: Trust or relationship depth must be built before a difficult ask, negotiation, or sale can be evaluated seriously.
The value offered first is real, relevant, and costly enough to signal sincere investment.
The goal is durable goodwill and mutual commitment, not one-off extraction of compliance.
do_not_use_when: Reciprocity pressure creates obligation without informed evaluation.
Gifts, favors, or access distract from the substance, economics, or ethics of the decision.
The exchange is being used to bypass deliberate judgment or create manipulative indebtedness.
case_evidence_needed: The ask, negotiation, or sale that needs trust before evaluation.
The value being offered first and why it is relevant.
The cost or commitment that makes the offer credible.
Evidence that the recipient can still evaluate the merits independently.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to name the value offered, test whether it is costly and relevant, and protect the recipient's ability to evaluate the actual decision.
evidence_required: The proposed gift, favor, work, or concession.
The recipient's actual problem or need.
The cost or sincerity signal behind the value.
The substantive decision criteria that must remain independent.
good_output_shape: Blocks obligation pressure from substituting for evaluation.

diagnostic_questions: What real value has been given before the ask?
Is the value relevant and costly enough to signal sincere investment?
Would the recipient still say yes if no obligation pressure existed?
misuse_guards: Do not use reciprocity to manufacture mindless compliance.
Do not let gifts or favors distract from substance, economics, or ethics.
Do not confuse goodwill with proof that the underlying offer is good.

source_evidence.source_quote: trust or relationship depth must be built before a difficult ask, negotiation, or sale can even be evaluated seriously
real, relevant, and costly enough to signal sincere investment rather than manipulation theater
durable goodwill and mutual commitment, not a one-off extraction of compliance

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 7 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports a costly-value trust test and warns against obligation pressure and distraction from substance.
dropped_material: Did not extract all influence-principle allies as separate affordances.
Kept professional pledge and skin-in-the-game examples as supporting evidence for one card.
open_questions: Future packet review should test this card against persuasion and signaling when value-first behavior could be either trust-building or manipulative.

absence_records.reason: The source warns that reciprocity can create obligation before the recipient has evaluated the merits.
The source rejects letting gifts, favors, or access distract from the substantive economics or ethics of the decision.

source_evidence.source_quote: gifts, favors, or access become a distraction from the substance, economics, or ethics of the underlying decision
The more value you give, the more value you get

review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 7 source reading found strong reviewed depth for Reciprocity Principle. Extracted one costly-value trust test and rejected obligation pressure plus favor distraction.
dropped_material: No runtime, prompt, lane, packet, or user-facing change is implied.
open_questions: Later packet comparison should check whether this card helps receivers distinguish durable goodwill from compliance tactics.
